item.content:encoded:
I’m wearing my Camp Nimrod shirt right now.

Coincidence? I think not.

item.title: Novel Enough
item.content:encoded:
I'd really love if this article got more detail - like anything that could reverse hte progress, or any instances of people being reached out to by SCP-3776-1s unsuccessfully. For instance, I suppose the agent that interviewed an SCP-3776-1 was just luckily not one of the members that could end up being infected with belief in camp Nimrod?

Does that make any sense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is this a youth camp that eats the youth of humans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A compelling addition to the Class of '76 canon. Disturbing and inconclusive, but in a way that enhances the mystery rather than undercutting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When it mentions "corresponding physical degradation", is that referring to dementia symptoms or some other aspect of the article I missed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was great! The idea of objects inexplicably appearing that cause dementia is terrifying! Hell, dementia is scary anyway especially if you have experience of what it can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh hell yeah a new class of '76 skip!

█████ ████: Oh boy, those were the salad days. So many of them, that's where we started. Camp is like a habit, when you keep going back. A lot of kids started… inaudible… started using it. Like a camp. They blistered and wilted in the sun.

this hit me right in the MYSTERIOUS +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added in the scientific name, will go through and make the edits TL333s pointed out at some point in the near future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, caveat, it'd probably be good to include both the Latin name and the common name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If an individual other than the subject opens the box it will only contain ashes and a significant number of palmetto bugs.

Perhaps change "palmetto bugs" to somthing more scientific. Periplaneta americana would look better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was you, Roget! Wow! Incredibly well done! I personally really enjoyed the story involved with the Camp Councilor and who that girl might be to them. I felt bad for him, genuine emotions were felt when he kept repeating "Summer is ending".

I don't know what Class of '76 is, but if they're anything else like this, I'm hooked already!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Considerably more sinister than I think of Class of '76 skips being, but I'm glad to see it nonetheless. I have a large wad of corrections to throw your way.

+ Cuz you need to fix some shit
- I mean, geez

as-of-yet unfound

I'm not sure what 'unfound' means in this context. Maybe "unlocated"? Or something even more on-tone?

Testimony by these users describes the camp as having wooden cabins, being placed at the edge of a lake

Not sure why 'users' is used here instead of 'subjects'. Also, should be an 'and' between 'cabins' and 'being'.

Once contact is made, a sealed cardboard container will appear in the attic of the last building the subject's biological parents lived independently.

The word 'in' is missing in this sentence. I mean, a second 'in'. Also, what happens if the building doesn't have an attic? Like, say, an apartment building?

If an individual other than the subject opens the box

Comma needed. Also in the next sentence, at the same point.

I still love the palmetto bugs.

Depending on the individual's age

Comma, and an 'and' after 'reels'.

I don't feel "elucidating" is the right word; how about "ascertaining"?

In almost every case these individuals will be close to the subject in a geographic sense.

Comma after 'case', and you could save a little long-windedness by saying "will be geographically close to the subject".

Many of the SCP-3776 subjects are impoverished

Comma. Also, the effect isn't universal, but is it statistically significant? I find it odd to mention that many are addicts and then being all, "But it doesn't actually matter."

Foundation attempts to locate a physical location corresponding with the camp have not been successful.

This is kind of repeating information given previously. I'd just roll it into the next sentence.

Told stories to the littler things, the littler kids.

Okay, if there's any one thing I love about this article, it's this right here.

Hmm, and now, reading this interview, I'm starting to wonder if 'users' above wasn't intentional. What an unusual comparison to make, between memories of summer camp nostalgia and addiction! Also I appreciate how the description of mental degradation earlier doesn't quite do justice to what's happening to these people.

I've got to get here.

Just making sure this is not a typo.

I do wonder what they did with the interviewed guy afterward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of the Outdoor Ed "live-away-from-home-in-a-cabin" week I did in… man, sixth grade. So long ago.

The "old wounds from camp" phrase in particular stuck with me. I definitely had some good times at Outdoor Ed, but man… being away from home felt so strange, but when it came to go back home, I wanted to stay for another day, even though I didn't really have many friends to hang out with in sixth grade and I spent most of the free time at camp reading or writing in a journal.

+1 for memories, man…

item.title: Tags
item.content:encoded:
Some tags that seem relevant: class-of-76, clothing, cognitohazard, euclid, memetic, memory-affecting, mind-affecting, scp.

Would "compulsion" fit here? It seems like seeking out other camp members might be a compulsive activity.

Which of mind/memory-affecting should it be? Both?

Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The newest Class of 76 article, my first in awhile! Credit to mlister, NineVolt, Conwell, Hippo, and everyone else who gave me pointers on this when it was gestating. Hope you guys had a great summer ;)

Picture is a shirt owned by me and designed by my father
